result:
There is a moment, late at night, when the house has finally exhaled and the refrigerator stops its low humming, when I realize that the small red eye of the smoke detector is the only thing still watching me. I am barefoot in the hallway, testing floorboards like a burglar in my own life, trying to remember which ones creak so I won’t wake the children. Their dreams are loud enough without me clomping through them. I carry a glass of water that has warmed to the temperature of my own blood, and I think: this is what it means to be a custodian of souls—you learn to move as quiet as fog so their sleep can stay a sovereign country.  

I used to believe that adulthood would feel like climbing a granite staircase where each step had a name: Diploma, Lease, Marriage, Mortgage, Promotion, Pension. Instead it feels like treading water in the dark, occasionally touching a step that crumbles the moment I trust it with my weight. The names dissolve; the water stays. Some nights I float on my back and count satellites instead of sheep, pretending their slow drift is a promise that something up there is still on schedule.  

My grandmother kept a shoebox of “just in case” items under her bed: a new roll of tape, half a chocolate bar, two Canadian dollars, a single nylon stocking still folded like a white flag. She died at ninety-three and when we emptied the house we found twenty-three identical shoeboxes, each labeled in her shaky fountain-pen architecture: “For Later.” I took one home and slid it beneath my own bed. It has become a portal; some evenings I lift the lid and inhale the cedar-paper ghost of her patience. I have added a glue stick, a marble my daughter swears is a planet, and the hospital bracelet from the night my son learned how quickly lungs can tighten into fists. The box is heavier now, but not with things—with hesitation. I understand that I am saving up for a future whose address keeps changing.  

When I was twelve I watched a thunderstorm split the sky above our subdivision like a seam ripper tearing velvet. The oak in the front yard took the bolt and for one impossible second the whole tree glowed, every leaf a filament. Then darkness stapled itself back together and the oak stood there smoking, leaves already curling into black commas. In the morning the branches looked normal until you touched them and they disintegrated into piles of carbon copy. I kept a fragment in a jelly jar, a pencil-thin twig that wrote gray streaks across the carpet whenever I sneaked it out to look. I wanted proof that something could be transfigured and still remain, even if it left smudges. Years later, during the first ultrasound of my first pregnancy, the technician swiveled the screen and I saw the pulse inside the pulsing, a strobe so fierce it could have been miniature lightning. I thought of the oak. I thought: we are all repositories of struck matches.  

I write sentences on the backs of receipts because groceries evaporate but words leave oil slicks on the mind. Yesterday’s says: “The quietest grief is the grocery store at 9 a.m., vast aisles of color-coded abundance while you try to remember if he liked raisins.” I will find that scrap in a coat pocket next winter, probably when I am late for a parent-teacher conference and the coat is too thin for the weather. The sentence will ambush me, and for a moment the whole parking lot will smell like his shirtsleeves.  

People keep asking when I will turn these scraps into a book. I tell them books need vertebrae, a spine strong enough to bear weight. My scraps are jellyfish—translucent, stinging, impossible to shelve. They float in the Mason jar on my desk, tentacles tangling around expired movie tickets and that fortune I got twice: “You will soon be crossing great water.” The first time I read it I was twenty, in love with a woman who lived on the other coast. The second time I was forty, divorcing her over video chat while our children slept under two different roofs. Great water keeps renegotiating its definition.  

Sometimes I imagine a parallel life where I never stopped moving. In that life I own one bowl, one spoon, one language at a time. I wake in hostels and drink coffee so strong it tastes like rusted bells. I do not know the names of local weeds, so nothing reminds me of childhood. My griefs are portable: a single backpack whose zippers never catch. Whenever I read about digital nomads I feel a homesickness for that life, the same reflexive ache I feel when overhearing someone order the dish I didn’t choose at a restaurant. Then one of my real children coughs in the night—a tiny, wet sound like a stapler misfiring—and the parallel life folds itself smaller than a boarding pass and drifts away.  

We are told to curate our days, to harvest joy like strawberries: pick only the red, leave the white shoulders for tomorrow. But joy is not the only fruit. There is also the purple bruise of uncertainty, the green grain of boredom, the yellow waxy buildup of routine. I eat them all because starvation is a sharper grief. My tongue has learned to translate even the bitter into energy.  

On the worst day of last year I stood in the shower until the hot water exhausted itself. I watched the cyclone of steam spiral down the drain and felt my chest cave inward like a demolished stadium. I could not locate a single reason to exit the tub. Then the cat scratched at the door, not plaintively but with entitlement, as if she had scheduled an appointment I had forgotten. I wrapped myself in a towel the color of over-washed denim and opened the door. She trotted in, tail semaphore-ing disdain, then paused to rub the side of her face against my wet calf. Her whiskers left a constellation of droplets that evaporated almost immediately, yet I can still feel them if I close my eyes. Reasons are overrated; sometimes momentum wears whiskers.  

I used to think the goal was to become unbreakable—some diorama of a person sealed under plexiglass, safe from dust and time. Now I suspect the goal is to break often and cleanly, like sea glass, so the edges soften and the light can pass through. I collect my own shards in a candy tin shaped like a merry-go-round. When it rattles it sounds like applause from a very small audience.  

Tonight the children sleep with their mouths open, exhaling stories I will never read. My partner’s phone glows under the pillow, a blue lighthouse signaling other adults who are also keeping vigil against tomorrow. I sit at the kitchen table and write this, one word at a time, like laying bricks for a house I will never finish. Outside, the neighbor’s motion-detector light keeps snapping on for nothing—wind, maybe, or a raccoon, or the ghost of that oak I still carry in a jelly jar. The light flares, then dims, then flares again, a Morse code I have not learned to read. I decide it is saying: “Keep going.” Or perhaps: “You are already home.”

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
